title: Cat Open Fontanel: Understanding the Soft Spot on Your Cat's Head
meta_desc: Learn about open fontanels in cats, whether a soft spot is an emergency, and how to safely monitor your kitten's skull development.
content_html:
What is an Open Fontanel?

An open fontanel is a physical gap in a cat's skull where the bony plates have not yet fused together. This creates a "soft spot" on the top of the head, similar to what is seen in human infants. While most kittens are born with these gaps, they typically close as the kitten matures. In some cases, particularly in certain breeds or due to developmental delays, the spot may remain open into adulthood.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for an open fontanel is typically Low. On its own, a soft spot is not a medical emergency; however, it does mean the brain has less protection against physical trauma. You should seek veterinary advice if the soft spot is accompanied by bulging, or if your cat shows neurological symptoms like circling, pressing their head against walls, or seizures. Otherwise, it is a condition that can be monitored during your next routine vet visit.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a clear, overhead photo of your cat's head can be incredibly helpful for a veterinary triage team. A photo allows professionals to visualize the size and exact location of the fontanel. It also helps them determine if there is any visible swelling, redness, or skin abnormalities in the area. Keeping a photographic record over time is also a great way to track whether the gap is shrinking as your kitten grows.

faqs.question: Will the soft spot ever close?
faqs.answer: In many kittens, the skull bones will eventually fuse and the fontanel will close within the first few months of life. If it remains open past six months, it may stay that way permanently.

faqs.question: Are certain breeds more prone to this?
faqs.answer: Yes, brachycephalic (flat-faced) breeds are statistically more likely to have persistent open fontanels due to the shape of their skulls.

faqs.question: How should I handle a cat with an open fontanel?
faqs.answer: You should be extra cautious to prevent any head trauma. Avoid rough play with other pets and ensure the cat does not fall from significant heights, as the brain is more vulnerable in that specific area.
